And the company could also enter into partnerships with other big-box retailers besides Target and set up pop-ups in their stores as well. Of course, I’m talking about events that might happen far in the future here, so this is speculative. But if you’re buying Warby Parker’s stock because it will eventually have 900 stores, you might want to consider that it could expand beyond that.

The reason why this matters is that right now many investors believe that Warby Parker is overvalued. The bear case for Warby Parker is that its stock is too expensive right now even though its expansion strategy is working. In fact, back in August, one of the reasons why I recommended buying National Vision Holdings was that it had a much lower valuation than Warby Parker. Even after National Vision Holdings’ recent rally, this is still true. But Warby Parker has a high valuation because investors believe in its growth potential, so I decided to check if this bull case was justified.

Warby Parker’s Stock Is Still Expensive

According to Seeking Alpha, Warby Parker gets D- or F grades on almost every valuation metric right now. Of course, this is in comparison to other specialty retail stores and not eyeglass stores specifically, and eyeglass stores have higher margins. So I’ll compare Warby Parker to the other eyeglass stores. After the market close on September 25, Warby Parker had a forward EV/sales ratio of 3.82 and a forward EV/EBITDA of 33.57. This is still high in comparison with National Vision Holdings, which had a forward EV/sales ratio of 1.49 and a forward EV/EBITDA of 15.68. And EssilorLuxottica, the global leader in the eyeglass business, had a forward EV/sales ratio of 4.91 and a forward EV/EBITDA of 19.23.

These metrics don’t make Warby Parker a bad deal by themselves, but it does mean that it’s necessary to justify the company’s high valuation. Additionally, it’s important to note that because the optometry market is so fragmented, all of these companies can grow market share by taking business from independent optometrists as well as each other. So I’m not changing my view on National Vision, but I also think Warby Parker can grow rapidly right now.

Warby Parker Now Has Wealthier Customers

Management also went into more detail about how the company’s plans are going at the Goldman Sachs conference. According to co-founder Neil Blumenthal, higher-income customers are still shopping at Warby Parker and many of them are repeat customers. This is important because many investors are concerned about the overall health of the economy right now. Shoppers are cutting back on unnecessary expenses. But if your prescription changed or your glasses broke you need to get new glasses, so this store sells necessary products. Of course, paying for them could be another issue.

While many eyeglass purchases are covered by insurance, insurance cuts are also a concern. For example, Federally Qualified Health Centers provide eye exams for low-income patients and Medicaid cuts will reduce their funding. While Warby Parker still sells entry-level glasses to low-income customers who might be affected by federal insurance cuts, it’s also attracting higher-income customers with insurance plans that can cover more expensive glasses.

By appealing to wealthier customers, Warby Parker has also been able to raise its prices, which has helped it absorb the costs of building brick-and-mortar eyeglass stores. Back in 2019, when it only operated as a DTC store, Warby Parker had a 60 percent gross margin. As an eyeglass store with an expanding physical presence, it has a gross margin of 55 percent in 2024. Its EBITDA is now positive and its net income is expected to be positive in 2026 as well.

Store Expansion Strategy

Warby Parker also plans to continue adding stores in its existing markets. It isn’t worried about cannibalization, and that makes sense. I’ve seen LensCrafters stores in malls in nearby cities before, and of course there are many other independent optometrists with practices in those cities. Eyeglass stores also market themselves based on convenience by promising to make your lenses quickly. During the Goldman Sachs interview, management even explained Warby Parker has added stores in 220 markets in the US, but it only has 1 store in 190 of those markets.

Another factor that some investors may not have considered is that because Warby Parker is growing its store count this quickly, it has more earning power than its financial reports imply. Warby Parker is building relatively small stores; Sachse Construction built several stores with footprints of between 940 to 1,800 square feet for the company. But even small eyeglass stores need to stock frames and lenses, buy equipment, and pay optometrists and other staff. While Warby Parker reported a loss of $20.39 million last year, it also added 41 stores. FinModelsLab estimates that it could cost $485,000 to $1.225 million to open an optical shop. And Warby Parker gives its optometrists and lab employees stock as well and includes those expenses in its gross margin, according to its earnings call. So this company’s brick-and-mortar store model is profitable, it’s just investing in expansion.

Valuation for Warby Parker

If you take the midpoint of its guidance, Warby Parker expects $884 million in revenue and $99.5 million in adjusted EBITDA this year. Its revenue will grow by about 14.5 percent and its EBITDA margin will be about 11.25 percent. It’s also expecting 14 percent growth in store count. So if Warby Parker adds another 45 stores next year, it will have 366 stores. That’s a 12 percent growth rate, so you could project positive comps and estimate 12.5 percent revenue growth. Warby Parker’s estimated revenue for 2026 would be $995 million.

Warby Parker’s EBITDA margin rose from 10.4 percent to 11.7 percent year-over-year in the second quarter. That’s 130 basis points. During the Goldman Sachs call, cofounder Neil Blumenthal explained that the company is trying to raise its margin by 100 to 200 basis points per year and has a long-term goal of achieving a 20 percent EBITDA margin. The midpoint of this estimate is 150 basis points. So you can project that in 2026, Warby Parker will have an EBITDA margin of 12.75 percent and an EBITDA of $127 million.

Warby Parker currently has a forward EV/EBITDA ratio of 34. Its EV is $3.38 billion and this includes $228 million in debt, $286 million in cash, and a $3.44 billion market cap. If you divide $3.38 billion by $127 million you get a forward EV/EBITDA ratio of 27, which is lower than analysts’ estimate. Warby Parker’s EV would have to increase to $4.26 billion to reach an EV/EBITDA ratio of 34. So this company’s estimated market cap for 2026 would be $4.32 billion.

You also have to consider a company’s share count before calculating the stock price. Warby Parker had 120.4 million shares outstanding at the start of 2024. It currently has 121.7 million shares outstanding. It issues shares to compensate its optometrists and other staff and its share count rose by 3 million from 2023 to 2024. So you can estimate that it would have a share count of 126.4 million at the end of 2026. If I divide the market cap of $4.26 billion by this share count, that’s a price of $34 per share. The closing price on September 25 was $27 per share so this would be 26 percent growth.

SA analysts and Wall Street analysts both rate Warby Parker a buy right now, although Wall Street analysts have a price target of $26. The SA quant rating is hold.

Conclusion

While Warby Parker’s stock does have a high valuation right now, this valuation is justified. This company is rapidly building brick-and-mortar stores and its margins are rapidly rising as well. It has introduced higher-priced products that appeal to wealthier customers, and its customers need the products it sells. It’s also coming up with additional growth drivers such as its Target partnership. So I would rate Warby Parker a buy.
